schools.summary: 'His own city' = Capernaum (his settled home, though born at Bethlehem, raised at Nazareth); the Synoptics differ in order; one history told by three.
Christ recrosses, leaving the Gadarenes who rejected him: he tarries not where unwelcome, forsaking in judgment those who slight him.
Mystically returns to 'the people of the faithful' by the boat of the Church; the Creator deigned to have an earthly country as man.
Teaching in a crowded house; the bearers uncover the roof and let down the pallet; 'seeing their faith' = both the man's and the bearers', shown by their labour.
The paralytic, anxious for body not soul, is first given pardon; 'son' to one the priests scorned; diseases often the punishment of sin.
Christ grants to their faith the favour he bestows; pardon pronounced first declares him author of the deeper deliverance.
The charge echoes John 5:18; rests on the true principle 'who can forgive but God only?'; 'blaspheme' = to speak impiously of God or usurp his prerogative; the sneer 'this fellow.'
Slander drove them to the charge; had they thought it blameworthy they would have inquired, not condemned in secret.
Knowing/seeing their thoughts proves deity, for God alone searches hearts (1 Chr 28:9; Jer 17:10; Jn 2:24); the thoughts 'evil' because they assumed wanton blasphemy.
A proof of Divinity by his Spirit (1 Cor 2:11); their offense is proudly rejecting God openly manifested.
Both sayings require unlimited power; easier to SAY the unverifiable pardon, harder to risk the visible cure, which Christ utters without fear.
Quickening a near-dead body by a word is no easier than forgiving; Christ adapts to their carnal capacity, moved more by outward signs.
Both argue Divine power; if he can heal the disease (the consequence of sin) he can forgive the sin.
The healing proves Messianic power/authority (exousia) on earth to forgive (cf. Jn 5:27); three interlocking miracles; the narrator's words interjected.
Christ is not minister but author of pardon, unlike pastors who only declare it; forgiveness exhibited on earth, not sought from afar.

outliers.note: Stray fragment on Christ's willing passion, unrelated to the verse.

schools.summary: Instant, complete cure: he rose, walked unaided, and carried home the bed that had borne him, glorifying God.
Mark's 'immediately' and Luke's 'glorifying God'; the exultant joy of a soul freed from sin and a body made new — the unanswerable proof demanded.
Best reading 'they were afraid' (cf. Mk/Lk); 'power unto men' — the crowd rested in a delegated authority, short of the union of manhood and Godhead in Christ.
To glorify God = to acknowledge his power; the words record their feelings, not the Evangelist's view of Christ's nature; the scribes' scandal is the people's praise.
Matthew = Levi (son of Alphaeus), two names; at the Capernaum tax-office; his humility in naming himself the publican; Jerome answers Porphyry/Julian.
The power of him that called rescued Matthew from evil, as Paul; called when assured he would yield.
A striking instance of grace: one from an infamous trade made not only disciple but apostle.
A 'great feast' (Lk 5:29) at Matthew's house, a farewell to old friends; his humble omission of 'great'; a strange medley of guests; publicans the despised tax-gatherers.
Matthew sought to bring his old associates to hear Christ, not despairing of them, as those brought to Christ desire others brought too.
To eat with men denotes intimacy; the scribes from Jerusalem watch outside and accuse Christ of being the companion of the wicked; the Pharisee feels no debt to mercy.
A stratagem aimed at the disciples, to draw them off by reproaching the Master with what seemed base.
A current proverb: the physician's place is among the sick; spoken with grave irony from the Pharisees' self-estimate of being whole.
The scribes' double error: ignoring Christ's office and despising others while sparing their own vices; the deadly disease of feeling whole.

outliers.note: Note here is mis-attached, on the cleansed leper of ch. 8.

schools.summary: 'Go and learn' a rabbinic idiom; Hos 6:6 'mercy not sacrifice' = mercy preferred to ceremony; came to call sinners (with studied reference to the Pharisees' self-estimate).
Christ the physician wounded for our sins; 'the whole' = those clinging to their own righteousness (Rom 10:3); no man righteous by law.
They contend with God and the Prophet when offended at mercy shown the wretched.

themes: I will have mercy not sacrifice

schools.summary: John's disciples a separate body with their own fasts (Pharisaic pattern); the Pharisees fasted twice weekly (Lk 18:12); Matthew/Mark/Luke harmonized, the questioners acting together.
A boastful ostentation of fasting; John's disciples blamed for siding with the Pharisees their master had condemned.
A stratagem to produce a quarrel between John's disciples and Christ's; cavils occasioned profitable discourse (Henry).
Christ the Bridegroom (Jn 3:29); the wedding guests cannot mourn while he is present; they shall fast when he is taken away (the passion).
The apostles, born of the spiritual marriage, joy in the Bridegroom's presence and the holy food; fast in his absence.
Christ apologizes for his disciples on the score of the season, sparing them while he is with them.

outliers.note: Stray fragment on the Eucharist (water to wine, wine to blood), unrelated to the verse.

schools.summary: Raw unfulled cloth shrinks and tears a worse hole; a fitness of things; the worn dress of the guests may have suggested the figure.
The old garment = disciples not yet renewed; the new patch = the new grace/fasting, not to be imposed lest they be broken by its severity.
Refers to the weak, tender disciples and a discipline too strict, not to the Pharisees; young converts to be tenderly dealt with.
'Bottles' = wineskins; old dry skins crack under fermenting new wine; the new wine = the Spirit's new energies (as at Pentecost).
Read of tender disciples and a stricter discipline (Calvin), or of the irreconcilable old and new covenants/self-righteousness (Clarke, Gill).

debates.question: Do the figures contrast tender disciples or the two covenants?

sides.position: the weak disciples and a too-strict discipline
the old (Pharisaic/legal) and new (gospel) systems, which cannot combine

schools.summary: Jairus, ruler of the synagogue, his only daughter of twelve; 'even now dead' / 'at point of death' / 'a-dying' harmonized by a messenger's news; Matthew's order preferred.
Christ's prompt, humble rising; allegory of Jairus as Moses/Law and the daughter as the synagogue in its twelfth year, brought to Christ for life.
A miracle sought by a synagogue ruler amid great mourning, which discomfited the Pharisees; the death perhaps exaggerated to gain compassion.
Christ rose at once, not upbraiding the ruler nor noting his weak faith (thinking presence and touch needful), but graciously following.
Though able to heal at a distance, he goes to the place, teaching ministers to spare no pains for a soul, to go to the bedside, not only pray in the closet.
An unclean disease (Lev 15:25) endured twelve years; she shrinks in shame and touches the fringe/tassel (Num 15:38); her twelve years match the girl's age.

The long, incurable, vainly-treated disease makes the instant cure shine; her thought of touching the garment, an extraordinary impulse, not a general rule, with an element of superstition Christ yet honours.
A faith real but weak: believing the power, not the love, thinking of a physical effluence; yet accepted (cf. Acts 19:12); modesty and uncleanness drove her to act in private.
She reasoned and firmly believed, moved by the Spirit and encouraged by reports of cures by touch.

themes: if I but touch his garment

schools.summary: Turning shows omniscience; the tender 'daughter, be of good comfort'; 'thy faith hath saved thee' in both the lower and higher sense.
Christ could not be defiled but removed her impurity; the exhortation corrects the weakness of her faith, yet commends it; faith needs forgiveness and new aid.

themes: thy faith hath made thee whole

schools.summary: Hired flute-players and mourning women (Jer 9:17); even the poorest afforded pipes; the tumult itself proof of death; Christ delayed that the miracle be evident.
Allegory: the ruler's house = the synagogue, the dead maid the soul dead in sin; minstrels = flatterers; worldly cares must be put out before the soul rises.
He put out the pipers but took in the parents, lest it be said he healed by other means.
'Sleep' a common Jewish term for death; Christ speaks in opposition to their opinion (perhaps Sadducean), affirming she shall rise as from sleep; kategelōn = loud scornful laughter.
A temporary suspension of sense, rightly called sleep, for she is not to remain under death's power.

outliers.note: Stray fragment on the Pharisees and Sadducees seeking a sign, unrelated to the verse.

schools.summary: Only five impartial witnesses admitted; a direct, complete raising; the crowd put out for secrecy and as unworthy; an emblem of raising the soul dead in sin.
Christ raised three (the child, the widow's son, Lazarus): no degree of death past his help.
Though he charged silence, the miracle could not be hidden; neighbours who knew her dead, seeing her alive, spread the fame everywhere.
Jesus scarcely appears; the work alone is manifested — the preacher should conceal himself that God have the glory; a proper miracle of unlimited power.
'Son of David' = the Messiah (Isa 9:7; Mt 1:1), used again by the Canaanite woman and the blind at Jericho; an importunate cry for mercy.
A charge against the Jews: the blind believe by hearing while the seeing will not; Christ leads them to a house to heal privately, shunning fame.
The two blind = Jews and Gentiles; the title proves Christ's real birth of David's line (against Marcion/Manichaeus); confession unto salvation (Rom 10:10).

outliers.note: Stray fragment on the oath to David and Christ's lineage, unrelated to the verse.

schools.summary: He passes them by to try their faith and avoid the crowd; in the house he draws out their explicit belief in his power, faith being the antecedent of the miracle.
Knowledge of his power is cold without conviction of his willingness; yet Christ applauds their faith, for they had owned him Son of David and Redeemer.

themes: believe ye that I am able

schools.summary: The touch is a sign of grace and a help to the blind, who could not see the Healer's face (cf. Jn 9:6); 'according to your faith' proportions the gift.
A general doctrine: pray in faith and never meet refusal; much more the faith of those endued with the Spirit of adoption.
Instant cure; 'straitly charged' (enebrimēthē, a snorting/anger word) = a vehement charge to restrain their joy; silence to avoid acclaim (Isa 42:2).
A model: servants should desire their good deeds hid, yet have them known of compulsion, that others profit.
Some reason for the silence unknown to us; the men, through inconsiderate zeal, spread it before the time, worthy of blame.

outliers.note: Note here is mis-attached, on the scribe of v.19.

schools.summary: The old question whether the zeal was praiseworthy: most patristic/Roman writers affirm, most Protestants deny; the latter judged 'ethically truer' — to obey is better than sacrifice (1 Sam 15:22); yet honour pursues those who fly from it.
Gill records simply that the two healed men, departing the house and city, published the report in other parts.

debates.question: Was the men's disobedient zeal in spreading his fame praiseworthy?

holders: most patristic and Roman Catholic commentators

sides.position: a fault — obedience to Christ's word would have been better

schools.summary: kōphon ('blunted'), used of deaf or dumb; the dumbness a spiritual disease, not congenital, the cure restoring sanity (cf. Mt 12:22; Lk 11:14); Augustine distinguishes this from the later like miracle.
Remigius marks the 'beautiful order' fulfilling Isa 35:6 ('the tongue of the dumb shall be loosed'); Rabanus reads the dumb demoniac, like the blind, as fallen humanity needing health in every part.
Chrysostom: the man, his spirit chained with his tongue, could ask nothing and was brought by others; Christ requires no faith of him but heals immediately.
Calvin: not naturally dumb but deprived of speech by the devil; Clarke's emblem — he who renders God no praise is possessed of a dumb demon.

The cause removed, the man spake; the miracle a demonstrative proof of Messiah in the flesh (Isa 35:5–6); the poor and simple readier than the rich and learned to own God's hand; Lightfoot refers the words to the whole day's works.
Hilary reads the dumb demoniac as the Gentile world, freed by the knowledge of God from the frenzy of superstition; the multitude's wonder is the confession of the nations.

'The prince of the devils' = Beelzebub (Mt 12:24); the charge current before the mission of the Twelve (Mt 10:25); unable to deny the miracles, the Pharisees discredit them by linking Christ to the devil; a consummate malice springing from envy.
Calvin sets the people's confession against the Pharisees' blasphemy: when wickedness reaches the height of blindness, no work of God, however evident, escapes perversion.

Imperfect 'went about' = a third Galilean tour; all but identical with Mt 4:23, opening the section leading to the discourse of Mt 10; 'every sickness' = every type, not every case; the example of an unwearied minister (Clarke).
Henry treats it as the point to be proved — that Christ preached and healed — now shown by an induction of particulars.

Chrysostom: having suffered blasphemy he does not punish but shows kindness, going about their towns — teaching us to return kindness; Jerome: he preaches in villages, cities, and towns, regarding the salvation of believers, not the might of the noble.
Calvin: the verse anticipates an objection, telling us his whole ministry is not minutely described, for he was constantly employed proclaiming and confirming the gospel.

esplanchnisthē (from the bowels) = a vehement commiseration; the better reading 'harassed and cast down' (eskylmenoi kai errimmenoi) — bewildered and laden by those who should teach (Mt 23:4); like sheep without a shepherd; same compassion in Mt 15:32.
Remigius reads in Christ the good shepherd's disposition, not the hireling's, healing souls as well as bodies.
Calvin infers the indolence of the priests, 'dumb dogs' (Isa 56:10), none of whom Christ owns as pastor; Wesley: the people faint in soul, with many teachers but none who cared for their souls.

A ripe field inviting reapers and demanding haste; the harvest = the multitude prepared to receive the gospel, the labourers few; rabbins/students called reapers; Ellicott finds a touching instance of Christ's human emotion craving his disciples' sympathy and prayer.

Calvin: by the harvest he intimates many are ripe — the elect mixed with unbelievers, valued above the rest; many assumed the teacher's character but few discharged it faithfully. Wesley: Christ's coming was the time of harvest, the few labourers those God sends to convert sinners.

'Send forth' (ekballō) = thrust/push out, for the office is so full of reproach, labour, and danger that nature is averse and God must push a man into it; the fittest are most backward; the minister a day-labourer come to work, not to lord; 'how seldom we pray for more preachers.'

Gill: the Lord of the harvest is the Father (whose are the elect) or Christ himself, who sends labourers — a proof of prayer made to Christ; Calvin: since no man becomes a faithful minister of himself, when pastors are scarce we must raise our eyes to him for the remedy, never more needed than in the church's desolation.

themes: pray the Lord of the harvest
